title: Read the active portal
question: If portal_side is 3, which chute does this program choose?
boardHint: Lane numbers stay simple: 1 = left, 2 = center, 3 = right.
unlockConnection: A variable can copy the visible portal_side value and reuse it for chute choice.
successMessage: Correct. target becomes 3, so the machine chooses the right chute.
failureMessage: target copies portal_side. If portal_side is 3, the program chooses the right chute.

title: Copy the active side
question: Which line should replace ??? so the program follows the active portal side?
boardHint: portal_side already stores the current left-or-right value.
unlockConnection: Variables let you store the board value first and pass it into choose_chute(...) after that.
successMessage: Correct. target = portal_side copies the current portal side before choose_chute(...) uses it.
failureMessage: If you want to follow the active portal, target should copy portal_side directly.

title: Repair the chute value
question: Which replacement fixes the program so it follows the active portal safely?
boardHint: choose_chute(...) must stay between chute 1 and chute 3.
unlockConnection: This repair checks whether you understand that portal_side already contains the value you need.
successMessage: Correct. portal_side already has the chute value, so the program can use it directly.
failureMessage: portal_side already stores the current side. Adding to it can push the chute choice out of range.

title: Read the next ball
question: If next_ball is evil_ball, what happens here?
boardHint: skip_ball() consumes the previewed ball without launching it.
unlockConnection: Conditionals matter here because the machine can react to the next ball before it enters the board.
successMessage: Correct. The condition is true, so the evil ball is skipped.
failureMessage: When next_ball matches evil_ball, the if-branch runs and skip_ball() consumes that ball.

title: Choose the correct condition
question: Which condition should skip only evil balls?
boardHint: The program should compare the preview value to the evil-ball constant.
unlockConnection: A useful if-statement checks the previewed ball type before deciding what the machine should do.
successMessage: Correct. next_ball == evil_ball skips the bad ball and keeps the other ones.
failureMessage: The condition needs to read next_ball and compare it directly to evil_ball.

title: Repair the preview logic
question: Which replacement fixes the broken branch so the program skips evil balls and drops the others?
boardHint: The fix should skip evil_ball and keep a real launch in the else-branch.
unlockConnection: Repair tasks check whether your branch logic really matches the ball-preview system.
successMessage: Correct. The repaired version skips only evil balls and still launches the others through the chosen chute.
failureMessage: The fix must compare next_ball to evil_ball, skip that case, and still launch the other balls.

title: What does the helper call do?
question: What happens when the main program runs follow_portal()?
boardHint: The helper editor stores one named tactic, and the main editor can call it.
unlockConnection: Functions matter here because one reusable tactic can now launch a visible helper-colored shot.
successMessage: Correct. Calling follow_portal() runs that helper function once.
failureMessage: A helper call executes the helper body once at the call site.

title: Choose a reusable helper
question: Which helper body best stores a reusable 'follow the active portal' tactic?
boardHint: The helper should contain the tactic you want to reuse, not just one unrelated line.
unlockConnection: A good helper turns repeated board logic into one named tactic.
successMessage: Correct. That helper stores the full reusable tactic: choose the chute, then drop.
failureMessage: The helper should capture the actual repeated tactic, not just a fragment of it.

title: Repair the main-program call
question: Which line should replace ??? so the main program actually uses the helper function?
boardHint: The main editor still needs a function call to trigger the stored helper body.
unlockConnection: Helper-colored shots only appear if the main program truly calls the helper instead of skipping it.
successMessage: Correct. follow_portal() is the line that triggers the stored helper function.
failureMessage: To use a helper, the main program needs the helper name followed by parentheses.

title: Repeated helper calls
question: How many times does the helper run here?
boardHint: Everything inside the loop body repeats once per range value.
unlockConnection: Loops let one good helper function feed the machine several times in the same run.
successMessage: Correct. range(3) repeats the helper call three times.
failureMessage: The loop body runs once for each value in range(3), so the helper runs three times.

title: Choose the repeat count
question: Which range value should replace ? if you want four repeated shots?
boardHint: The number inside range(...) decides how many times the tactic repeats.
unlockConnection: Choosing the correct range is how a loop becomes a deliberate multi-ball plan instead of random repetition.
successMessage: Correct. range(4) repeats the helper function four times.
failureMessage: Use the exact number of repeats you want. Four repeats means range(4).

title: Repair the loop body
question: Which replacement fixes the code so the helper is repeated by the loop instead of running only once after it?
boardHint: The repeated action must be indented inside the loop body.
unlockConnection: Loop repairs check whether the repeated shots come from real repeated logic, not from one final call outside the loop.
successMessage: Correct. follow_portal() belongs inside the loop body so it repeats three times.
failureMessage: The loop body has to contain the repeated helper call, and it must stay indented under the for-line.
